sentences.text: It meant-- it meant really a lot.
It like become a-- a-- like a new hope.
There is somebody I can speak to who is your flesh and blood.
It gave me a little faith, awakening in myself, even if it was very temporary, that maybe there was a chance that someday maybe we will see freedom.
It was just a glimpse of such a thing.
But it awakened in me something that I was missing for the last seven weeks.
Because nobody really close to me from home was around me.
So finding them and seeing the fact that they're alive gave me like a little boost.
By that time, we were so dehumanized.
I tried to explain this many times to people that have no concept really what Birkenau or Auschwitz or a concentration camp as such was.
You cannot under circumstances that are beyond description of suffering-- you cannot be day and night hungry and thirsty and be sick, first of all, even maintain your normal way of thinking.
You lose-- your-- your brain like, slows down, or you become detached from the eye.
There is no such thing anymore that today, an intelligent person starts thinking in the realm of who am I?
What am I?
You lost your complete contact that there is such a thing as you, and God, and belief, and faith, because you are at the point that you see this is-- this is your end.
This is it.
Of course, you uttered the word 'oh God, help me.' As they say, there is no atheist in a foxhole.
And there was no such a thing as a person not being religious in a manner of thinking, oh God, help me.
Everybody was saying at one time or another oh God, help me.
But the last thing in the world you had on your mind was the concept of thinking oh, maybe today is Sabbath, and I'm not supposed to work.
That was so far away from your mind.
And I think, at least in the stage that I was, that I had lost the whole concept or the whole way of thinking of wait a minute, you didn't pray today.
You didn't-- it was completely lost.
It was just gone.
Because my main subconscious way of maybe one day going out from this calamity was what am I doing next?
Maybe not in these terms as I'm expressing them now, but that was all I was geared-- my body was geared to.
How do I find something to take in my mouth to sustain myself?
How do I escape and get in a place of work that I don't have to work so hard, where I can rest a little bit more?
In other words, everything was geared towards sustaining oneself and to see yet the evening or tomorrow.
At this point, in this stage where we are arriving now to talk about, I would say was one of the lowest points of the 37 months that I suffered.
Except later on in the end, as we will discuss later, that you saw people around you where you realized, from now on you are in hell.
You will never get out of here.
You saw people around you that had no more flesh left on their bodies.
You saw people, when you looked at them, all you saw was skeletons.
And how the skeletons really moved about on the ground, it was a miracle that a person like this can even breathe, can stay alive.
So this was like the last station of where a human can say, well, this is it.
If you ever get out from this kind of a position that we find ourselves now, maybe you will make it.

But the battle for survival went to an extent that I watched children who were near their father waiting that their parent would take the last breath so they can have their bread ration and-- and devour it.
It's-- it-- what a piece of bread, what a little water meant at this particular situation in Birkenau was beyond description.
Because we were subjected to the most cruel, the most inhumane treatment.
And what was hurting more was that fellow peers that were locked up with us did this to us.
Because they were given all the authority and all the power to do with us whatever they wanted to.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in a-- in a certain block.
I think the block was number 13.
We had a German blockalteste who was locked up even before-- in the early Hitler times, he was what they call in German a [GERMAN].
He used to have a triangle with a green marking on it that meant that he was a common criminal at home.
And these people were taken from Sachsenhausen, I think he came.
And they were made the blockalteste and the guide-- the leaders of us.

He would line up the people in the morning, take something that is common here in America, called maybe a baseball bat, and slug any kind of a person that he felt like it two times on the neck, one times on the back, and bingo.
The man was dead.
And he had to give no accounting to anybody as long he produced a dead body.
I mean, to watch these atrocities-- this dehumanizing of-- of day in and day out, this lack of-- of food-- I came to a point where watching a person die meant nothing.
It's like watching a fly go by today.
It probably hurts me more today when a fly dies than it hurt me at that time a human being dying.
It was so common.
It was-- it was in such multiples, and you saw it minute in and minute out.
We were going, I remember, the three brothers-- there was-- there was nothing left on us as far as weight.
I mean, we were all bones.
And at one time or another, we were all weak.
We were all-- we couldn't walk.
But we were like holding onto each other, one trying to give a little bit-- drag to the other one, so we can continue walking.
Because we knew the minute you let yourself go, and you're on the ground, you're out.
So it was a battle.
I remember standing in a camp in the morning by appelle.
When you count people, when you dish out the food, the abuse was constant.
Summer with the heat and winter with the cold, no protection whatsoever.
And if somebody was missing, or the count wasn't right, you could stand there for hours.
Bearing in mind, you're talking about people who are normally at this stage of the game not capable of doing anything.
It was really a miracle that-- that-- that all of us were not to taken with trucks and dumped into the mass graves and killed.
Because they had a-- a system.
Apparently, right now, whoever can still breathe and walk has to try to bury the other one who is already dead.
And we had different kind of jobs.
Not all the time where we were together.
We tried to, but it wasn't always working out.
So the-- most of the people at that time, the ones who did not run freely to the electric wires to take their lives-- and that itself took-- took also a certain amount of stamina to do.
But once you gave up-- and we have watched this day in and day out, many, many times.
But everybody-- not everybody is powerful enough or strong enough to-- to end his own life.
And remember that most of the people at that time were suffering from diarrhea, and that was probably like, like cholera, like you-- you-- you-- you're undernourished.
You haven't got any vitamins.
You haven't got any minerals in your body.
Your-- your-- your weight is diminished to almost nothing.
And then you develop diarrhea.
At that time in Auschwitz, almost everybody had that.
So once you had diarrhea, you haven't got the possibility to go to bathrooms, so you do it in your pants.
You're all wet, and the next time an SS man sees you, he knew right away, this is it.
And then they used to grab you off the walk during-- during the course of walking to work, from work.
If your-- if your pants were soiled, or you just accidentally fell down in the mud, he already assumed that you have the diarrhea, and you were gone.
You were a goner, period.
And at this particular time, I remember one day I developed diarrhea, myself.
And I was-- I was frantic.
I mean, I-- I knew this is-- this is the end of me.
And most of the time that I can recall is people with diarrhea-- the longest they ever lasted was three days.
First of all, you lost your appetite.
You didn't have-- you were dehydrated.
You didn't have what to drink.
You didn't have what to eat.
It didn't take, I would say, maximum three days.
So one day, I was in a working detail in the fields, and there was some Russians from the ones who were running the kitchens.
They had a lot of bodies, these Ukrainians that I mentioned.
They stole from the kitchen, or they took from the kitchen, potatoes.
And they made themselves a fire in the fields, and they made baked potatoes.
So they used wood to make the fire-- all kinds of wood that they found there.
And-- and I recalled as a child when I was at home, if I ever had diarrhea, there was certain black pill.
And my mother used to explain to me that it's made from wood, from charcoal.
I mean, I didn't exactly know what and when, but that pill would stop-- by a person at home, under normal circumstances-- it would stop the diarrhea.
So when I saw the coal, an idea came to my mind.
Don't forget, I'm-- I'm-- I'm what?
I'm 17 years of age.
I'm trying to be my own doctor in-- in Auschwitz.
And I see that coal.
So I took a few stones, and I tried smashing that coal, the charcoal to powder.
I don't know if you can picture what it means to take charcoal powder and start eating it, and there's no water.
And you take it in your, mouth and I forced it with all my power.
And I forced it, and somehow, three hours later, my diarrhea stopped.
It was a miracle.
I though to myself my God, I invented a medicine.
There's so many people dying from diarrhea.
I'm going to make a business out of this.
And as silly and ridiculous as it sounds now, but-- but I started-- I started taking the stuff.
And of course, you had no paper or nothing.
I stuffed it in my pants or wherever I could.
And I took stones, and I used to go around to guys who had diarrhea, and they couldn't eat anything.
I'd say, I'll trade you.
I'll give you medicine, and let me have your-- your ration of bread.
Or give me your coffee, and we'll trade then.
If he couldn't-- I'll stop your diarrhea.
And I used to sell this-- this charcoal for a while there, and for many of them, it helped.
How?
I don't know how.
But apparently, it must be something in the truth, but I know it helped for me.
The only problem was that immediately after that happened, I got detached from my two brothers, from the job, and they took me to a-- to a Sonderkommando.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I have to be explaining first what Sonderkommando meant in Birkenau at that time.
We have to be remember that at that time, they did not have any crematorium where they could burn people on a-- on a large scale.
So what they had is a detail which was called Sonderkommando 1, special detail 1.
Their job was to gas the people.
They took them in the-- by now everybody knows the showers where they had these fake things, and they used to throw in the Zyklon B, and they gassed the people.
And then they would take these people out, drag the bodies out.
Then the Sonderkommando 2 would come pick up these-- the people, and they would bury them in a mass grave.
So somebody had to dig these large, mass graves.
And that was Sonderkommando 3.
And they took me in that detail.
Of course at that time, I didn't have the slightest idea what I was doing.
As I mentioned to you before that my mind was completely out of the realm of a normal human being thinking what are you doing.
We were just going like-- like robots.
And I was working with a lot of guys, digging a large, large grave.
At that time, I didn't know it was a grave.
A large hole.
I mean, a humongous hole.
And a day or two days later, they brought us back.
That was a day shift.
And we found these large graves covered partially with some white lyme-- it was a certain disinfection-- and a little dirt.
I didn't have the slightest idea what is going on there.
And our job was now to complete covering that to the top.
And I used to come back to camp, and my brothers asked me, what are you doing?
I told them, I don't know.
I'm digging graves, and then I'm covering it.
So at the moments that we were able to think clear, he said, what are you talking about?
How can you dig a grave and then cover it?
I mean, I didn't say a grave.
Digging holes, because we didn't know it's a grave.
You're digging-- you're digging a hole, and then the next day you're going back.
You're covering it.
What's in the hole?
I said, nothing.
I see dirt.
So they started nudging me, nudging me, nudging me.
And my mind really didn't connect on what is going on.
And at that time, neither my brothers nor I realized that I'm in a-- in a dangerous field.

Because they used to-- every four to six weeks, they used to get rid of all these Sonderkommandos, because they didn't want, again, somebody running away and being able to tell the world what's going on there.
That was-- that was-- that was fearful for the Germans.
So one day, I don't know.
I was-- I was able to clear just -ly think for a second.
When I was covering this big hole, I saw an arm or a leg or something of a dead body.
And for a minute, I was thinking to myself, wait a minute.
I said, I'm burying dead people.
I didn't know from where, from what.
But I realized this is a mass grave.
So when I come back this evening to camp, I'm running to my brothers, and I'm telling them, hey, you know what I'm doing?
I'm doing this, and this, and this, and this.
He says, you better get out of this place.
You've got to get out of this place, otherwise you're not going to live.
Because they're doing away with this group every few weeks.
Everybody knows that these people don't live long.
Because people used to disappear.
Nobody knew how, when, and what.

So it wasn't so easy to get off from a detail either, but somehow I pretended a day or two days later, after I found that out, that I'm ill, and I have to go in a infirmary, which itself was very dangerous.
Because very few people came out from an infirmary in Birkenau.
And somehow I got out of this detail.
And-- and eventually I went-- I joined my two brothers and doing odd and ends with them together until such a stage that I really felt that I'm-- that I'm unable to do anything anyone.
And I told my brothers, I'm going to-- I'm going to go in the infirmary.
I can't-- I can't do it anymore.
I just-- I just-- I'm unable to do it.
And I used to get up in the night, and I had to go to the bathroom.
I had to-- I was dizzy.
I couldn't walk.
I had to hold onto the wall for a while until I could steady myself.
And I-- I just-- I just saw there's no end to it.
It's-- it came to a point where you didn't-- really didn't care.
As long as I don't have to go anymore to work, I-- I don't care.
Am I dead, am I alive.
I didn't probably think in those terms.
But I just didn't care anymore.
I just gave up in the inside of me.
My brothers warmed me from that.
And the next day, I went to the infirmary, and I reported sick.
They took me in there.
At least, for me, it was a relief.
I don't have to go to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So when my brothers found out that I'm in the infirmary, there was all kinds of ways to send messages.
They came running every day to the window there.
They couldn't come in because it was all gated up.
And they begged me I should get out there.
And I really felt so miserable.
I was, like, thankful to God that I can have a little rest there, then I have to go to work.

It sounded-- it sounded-- you know, it sounds funny right now, but to get a little relief of not having to go to work, and I got at least my cup of coffee in the morning and a piece of bread, that it was like the whole-- a whole new world opened up for me.

Except the danger that my brothers knew is that every time that this placed filled up, which took maybe-- I don't know, 1,000 maybe 1,500 people-- they used to come with big vans, and they used to load all these people up, and they used to take them to the crematorium to gas them and get rid of them.
I was-- I was too stupid at that time or losing my mind even to think of such a thing and probably so degraded by now in so far not caring that it just-- it just didn't matter.
Probably the same way as the other guy had the stamina to run to the wires and take his life, that was my was of saying, enough is enough.
I can't do it anymore.
And then one day, they were emptying out that infirmary.
And everybody had to go to the yard outside.
Of course, that was the day that they were going to get rid of them.
So as everybody walked out, there were two guys, also people who were from the concentration camp, asking you what's the matter with you.
So everybody was telling them what's wrong with them.
And they asked me what's wrong with me.
I told them that I get up in the night, and I'm dizzy.
And I have to hold on to a wall or to something until I steady myself.
And otherwise, I feel like falling down.
So I heard one telling the other guy that he thinks that I have typhus.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So one guy said, you know what?
You go back.
You go back inside to the bunk.
Lie down.
I did.
In the afternoon, I fell asleep.
It was in the morning.
I don't know-- 8:00, 9 o'clock or so.
In that afternoon, I got up.
The whole place is empty.
There's nobody there.
And I asked the guys who are in charge-- the [NON-ENGLISH] there.
I says, where is everybody?
So he looks at me, and he says, you don't know?
I said no, I don't know.
He says, they took them all-- they took them all to be gassed.
So it, like, shook me up.
And then I asked the guy whether he could go-- yeah, in the evening, my two brothers don't show up.
They don't show up because later on, they told me, one of them had a Jewish calendar somehow smuggled in.
They already put down the date of my death, because they head the whole place is emptied out, so I'm gone too.
So they put down the date when I died, in case they ever come out, so they have at least a date.
And I went over to the guy then, and I tell him, listen.
I'll give you my bread, my ration.
Go tell my brothers and ask them why they're not coming.
I didn't realize that-- that they already know that I'm not here anymore.
So he took my bread, and he never went to notify them.
Well, these blocks were not any further away than here houses are from each other.
So next day, I begged him and I cajoled him again, and again, and again.
So in the evening, he finally took away again my ration-- or the additive that was with the ration, maybe a piece of horse salami or whatever they gave you, and he went and he notified them.
I remember like today when my two brothers came to the window.
It was like they looked at somebody who just got up from the dead.
And they started crying and begging and saying, get out of here!
Get out of here!
I says, I feel a little better now.
I'm going to tell them, and I'm going to try to get out.
And sure enough, the next day, I really, you know, picked up a little strength.
And I was feeling a little bit better.
It was like a-- like a miracle that this guy takes my pulse, and he says, I think he has typhus, and he sends me back to the-- to the-- to the-- it was like an angel showed up there.
I mean, if I think back today, it's like-- it's like a guardian angel showing up and says, you go back inside.
If I would have stayed outside, I would really been gone.
And the next day, I told the guy that I feel much better, and they let me go out there again.
Then I joined my brothers again in the detail, at which time they came to camp with-- some SS guy came to camp with a civilian guy.
I still remember, his name was Mr.
Mueller.
He was an elderly gentleman, and they introduced him that he is a brick layer master.
Master bricklayer.
And he's looking for some young people whom he wants to train and give them schooling in brick laying.
So they tell us that whoever will volunteer will have a chance to be inside a building the whole day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they will teach us how to become masters in brick laying.
So I said, it's better than going out to work.
So I volunteered.
And my brother-- my middle brother-- also volunteered.
And they took us away from Birkenau to Auschwitz, to the main camp now.

And there, they erected in one of the blocks on the third floor a place as today as you would picture a basement in somebody's house, where they brought a lot of bricks-- dry bricks, no mortar-- and this civilian came in every day to give us lectures on how you're supposed to lay bricks.
Without any cement, just dry.
He showed how to make corners, how to make walls, and how to keep them straight, and how to pull a string across, and how to use a [NON-ENGLISH], a level-- which is mean.
And-- but we picked up a little strength.
It was-- it was like a relief from what we were going through up until now-- I mean, not a comparison.
First of all, in the place of work, you didn't get beat anymore.
Because this German civilian-- he didn't-- he might have yelled at us, but he did not beat anybody.
And we were no-- under no SS guard supervision inside the block.
So it was like going from-- from hell to paradise all of a sudden.
You had-- you get your-- you had your-- you got your ration of drinking, of your coffee or your tea.
You got your bread.
And we began picking up a little strength and putting on a little weight, if I can say so.
I mean, if you can picture today that-- that-- you know, when you're a skeleton, to put on a little weight under these conditions is very easy.
It doesn't take a lot.
But it was a big, big relief.
And I feel today that if it wouldn't have been for this chance, that my brother and I got in this place, that things probably would've ended up with me never coming out of there.
And little by little, we gained more and more strength.
And it came to a point where they took us out there from there now, from the Auschwitz camp, back to Birkenau, and put us now on the real buildings to start-- perform.
To do a job.
Now so the beatings now came-- started to beat again, because now we had already the old supervisors and so forth.
But by now, we had already-- we were strong enough again to be able to start anew to take all this-- this-- this subhuman treatment.
But it was much better than the period that I mentioned to you before with the-- with the Sonderkommando and so forth.

It's-- it's in comparison today, if you take somebody and take them out from a jungle, and you put them under a controlled environment where he still has-- not good, but it's much better than the previous experiences that you had.
And we kept on working on these buildings.

I would say that, in general, we probably had it a little bit better than the-- than the-- than the rest of the guys who had to go out in the fields and who were deprived of all kinds of food and so forth.
Because it helped us a lot that-- that I remember how many months we were there under this dry, what they call-- teaching in the building.
It gave us a lot of protection from weather and from rain and from so forth.
And you had quasi-decent kind of food, if you can say so, in a concentration camp.
But it gave us a new hope that maybe someday, who knows, you might see the daylights again out of this place.
